questions.title: Complete find_last Function
questions.description: Drag the correct tokens into the blanks to complete the find_last function that finds the last node with a matching key:
questions.explanation: We initialize r to NULL to track the last matching node. We traverse the list while node is not NULL, check if the current node's key matches k, and update r when we find a match. Finally, we return r which points to the last matching node (or NULL if none found).
hints: Remember that initialize r to NULL since we haven't found any matches yet
Consider using != to check if node is not NULL
Consider using -> to access struct members through a pointer
Remember that update r to point to the current node when keys match
questions.hint: Think about: we initialize r to null to track the last matching node.

questions.title: Simple Tree Node Count
questions.explanation: The tree has 3 nodes: root (n1) with two children (n2 and n3). count_nodes returns 1 + count_nodes(left) + count_nodes(right) = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3. Recursive functions call themselves. They need a base case to stop recursion and prevent stack overflow.
hints: Remember that count all nodes in the tree
Remember that root has two children
questions.hint: Think about: the tree has 3 nodes: root (n1) with two children (n2 and n3).

questions.title: Inorder Traversal Output
questions.explanation: Inorder traversal visits: left subtree, root, right subtree. The tree has structure 2(1,3), so inorder prints: 1, 2, 3. Recursive functions call themselves. They need a base case to stop recursion and prevent stack overflow.
hints: Remember that inorder: left, root, right
Remember that visit left child (1) first
Remember that then root (2), then right (3)
questions.hint: Think about: inorder traversal visits: left subtree, root, right subtree.

questions.title: Preorder Traversal Output
questions.explanation: Preorder traversal visits: root, left subtree, right subtree. The tree has structure 2(1,3), so preorder prints: 2, 1, 3. Recursive functions call themselves. They need a base case to stop recursion and prevent stack overflow.
hints: Remember that preorder: root, left, right
Remember that then left (1), then right (3)
questions.hint: Think about: preorder traversal visits: root, left subtree, right subtree.

questions.title: Postorder Traversal Output
questions.explanation: Postorder traversal visits: left subtree, right subtree, root. The tree has structure 2(1,3), so postorder prints: 1, 3, 2. Recursive functions call themselves. They need a base case to stop recursion and prevent stack overflow.
hints: Remember that postorder: left, right, root
questions.hint: Think about: postorder traversal visits: left subtree, right subtree, root.

questions.title: Tree Height Calculation
questions.explanation: The tree is a chain: 1->2->3. Height counts nodes from root to deepest leaf: 3 nodes = height 3. Recursive functions call themselves. They need a base case to stop recursion and prevent stack overflow.
hints: Remember that count nodes from root to leaf
questions.hint: Think about: the tree is a chain: 1->2->3.

questions.title: Tree Sum Recursive

questions.title: Calculate Struct Size
questions.description: What is sizeof(struct Example)? Assume: char=1, int=4, double=8, pointer=8 bytes
questions.explanation: With padding: char a (1 byte) + 3 bytes padding + int b (4 bytes) + char c (1 byte) + 3 bytes padding = 12 bytes total. The struct is padded to align to its largest member (int, 4 bytes). sizeof returns the size in bytes at compile time. For arrays, it only works on the original array, not a pointer parameter.

Remember that padding is added after 'a' and after 'c'

questions.hint: Think about what sizeof returns - the size in bytes, not the number of elements.

questions.title: Nested Struct Initialization
questions.explanation: O.in.b is 2, o.c is 3, so 2 + 3 = 5.
hints: Remember that nested braces initialize nested struct

questions.title: Offsetof and Alignment
questions.description: What does this program print? Assume char=1, int=4.
questions.explanation: Offsetof gives byte offset of a member. char a (1 byte) + padding (3 bytes) puts int b at offset 4.
hints: Remember that padding added after char a
questions.hint: Think about: offsetof gives byte offset of a member.

questions.title: Const Struct Members
questions.explanation: Cannot modify const member after initialization. Assignment to cfg.max_size is a compilation error.
hints: Consider const members cannot be modified
Remember that only initialized, never assigned
questions.hint: Think about: cannot modify const member after initialization.

questions.title: Empty Struct Initializer
options: Compilation error in C (OK in C++)
questions.explanation: Empty initializer {} is a C++ feature, not valid in C. In C, use {0} to zero-initialize. Always initialize variables before using them to avoid undefined behavior.
hints: Remember that c requires at least one value
questions.hint: Think about: empty initializer {} is a c++ feature, not valid in c.
